subject: Mathematics
q: Find the range of the data shown in the frequency polygon below.
skill: Measure range, variance and standard deviation
feedback:
First, we need to find the class intervals and the corresponding class boundaries for the data given in the frequency polygon. From the graph, the size of the class intervals is \[3\] (difference between adjacent midpoints).
Since the midpoints are \[3, 6, 9, 12\] and \[15\], the corresponding class intervals are \[2-4,\; 5-7,\; 8-10,\; 11-13\] and \[14-16\].



The first and last class intervals have no observations, so we ignore them. Therefore,
Range \[= \] difference between the two extreme observations \[=13.5-4.5=9\].

Important points:
Note that we use class boundaries in the calculation of range, and not class limits.
The points on the \[x\]-axis represent the midpoints, not the class boundaries.
Remember that the first and the last class intervals are added to make the frequency polygon a closed figure, so they have to be ignored in the calculation.

subject: Mathematics
q: The variance of a variable is defined as _____.
options: the positive square root of the mean of the squared deviations of observations from their arithmetic mean
the extent of variation between the two extreme observations in a dataset
the mean of the squared deviations of observations from their arithmetic mean
the most frequently occurring observation in a dataset
skill: Measure range, variance and standard deviation
feedback:
The variance is the mean of the squared deviations of observations from their arithmetic mean.
The positive square root of the mean of the squared deviations of observations from their arithmetic mean is called the standard deviation while the most frequently occurring observation in a dataset is called the mode of a dataset.
The extent of variation between the two extreme observations of a dataset is called the range of the dataset.

subject: Mathematics
q: \[4\] students are having a discussion about measures of dispersion. Which one of these statements is correct?
options: Andy: The variance of a variable can be negative or positive.
Brandon: The range does not depend on the number of observations.
Charles: The variance does not depend on the number of observations.
Dean: The standard deviation is the negative square root of the variance.
skill: Measure range, variance and standard deviation
feedback:
Since the range is the difference between the smallest and largest values in a dataset, it is only dependent on these \[2\] extreme values. The number of observations in the dataset does not influence the range.
Hence, Brandon's statement about the range not depending on the number of observations is correct.

The formula for variance is Var\[(x)=\frac{∑(X-\bar{X})^2}{n}\]
Since both the numerator and denominator cannot be negative, \[Var(x)\] cannot be negative either.

In the formula for variance, the number of observations, \[n\], appear in the denominator. This tells us that the variance does depend on the number of observations

The standard deviation is only the positive square root of the variance NOT the negative square root of the variance.

subject: Mathematics
q: The following diagram shows the standard position of angle \[x\]. What is a possible value of \[x\]?
skill: Measure an angle in degree, minute and second
feedback:
Since the terminal side has travelled in the clockwise direction from the initial side, we know that \[x\] must be negative. So, we can rule out \[220 ^\circ \] and \[160 ^\circ \].
Next, we see that the terminal side has travelled through more than \[2\] right angles. This means the magnitude of \[x\] must be more than \[180 ^\circ \] in the clockwise direction. Therefore, \[x=-200 ^\circ \].

subject: Mathematics
q: What is the standard position of \[-50 ^\circ\]?
skill: Measure an angle in degree, minute and second
feedback:
Note that \[-50^\circ\] is negative, so the terminal side has to rotate in the clockwise direction. Thus, the diagrams in which the angle rotates in the anti-clockwise directions can be ruled out.
We know that \[-50^0\] is smaller than \[-90^0\] in magnitude, so this angle must not go beyond the \[90 ^\circ\] mark.
The diagram below shows \[-50^\circ\] in standard position.

subject: Mathematics
q: How many seconds are there in one degree?
skill: Measure an angle in degree, minute and second
feedback: Since \[60\] seconds make one minute and \[60\] minutes make one degree, the number of seconds that make one degree is \[60 \times 60=3{,}600\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Which of the following diagrams below shows an angle that has a positive measure?
skill: Measure an angle in degree, minute and second
feedback: The angle is said to have a positive measure if the rotation of the ray is anticlockwise and negative measure when it is clockwise. The image below shows an angle that has a positive measure.

subject: Mathematics
q: An angle is said to be in standard position if _____.
options: its initial side is on the negative \[y-\]axis and its vertex is located at the origin
its initial side is on the positive \[y-\]axis and its vertex is located at the origin
its initial side is on the negative \[x-\]axis and its vertex is located at the origin
its initial side is on the positive \[x-\]axis and its vertex is located at the origin
skill: Measure an angle in degree, minute and second
feedback: An angle is said to be in standard position if its initial side is on the positive \[x-\]axis and its vertex is located at the origin.

subject: Mathematics
q: To get one degree, we divide the circumference of a circle into _____ equal arcs. The angle subtended at the centre of the circle by one arc is called one degree.
skill: Measure an angle in degree, minute and second
feedback: The circumference of a circle is divided into \[360\] equal arcs, which is why the angle subtended at the center of a circle is \[360^\circ\].

subject: Mathematics
q: An angle is the union of two _____.
options: collinear rays with no common end points
collinear rays with a common end point
non-collinear rays with a common end point
non-collinear rays with no common end points
skill: Measure an angle in degree, minute and second
feedback: An angle is defined as the union of two non-collinear rays with a common end point.

subject: Mathematics
q: Express the angle \[36.34^\circ\] in Degree-Minute-Second (DMS) notation.
skill: Convert an angle given in degrees, minutes and seconds into decimal form and vice versa

subject: Mathematics
q: Express \[16^\circ\ 40'\ 38''\] as degrees.
skill: Convert an angle given in degrees, minutes and seconds into decimal form and vice versa

subject: Mathematics
q: Convert \[118.32^\circ\] to degrees, minutes, and seconds.
skill: Convert an angle given in degrees, minutes and seconds into decimal form and vice versa

subject: Mathematics
q: Which of the following angles is written correctly?
skill: Convert an angle given in degrees, minutes and seconds into decimal form and vice versa
feedback:
Since a degree is divided into \[60\] minutes, and a minute is divided into \[60\] seconds, when writing an angle, both the minutes and seconds must be less than \[60\].

Therefore, \[41^\circ 10' 53''\] is the only angle that is written correctly in the \[D^\circ M' S''\] amongst all the mentioned angles.

subject: Mathematics
q: Which of the following angles is written in decimal form?
skill: Convert an angle given in degrees, minutes and seconds into decimal form and vice versa
feedback: The angles  \[20 ^\circ   45'  50''\], \[183^\circ   12'   37''\] and \[331^\circ   53'   5''\] are written in the \[D ^\circ   M'   S''\] form, while the angle \[248.78 ^\circ\] is written in the decimal form.

subject: Mathematics
q: Denoting degree as \[D\], minute as \[M\], and second as \[S\], what is the correct presentation of an angle in the sexagesimal system?
skill: Convert an angle given in degrees, minutes and seconds into decimal form and vice versa
feedback: In the sexagesimal system, an angle is presented in the form \[D^\circ   M'   S''\]. As an example, \[5^\circ   10'   20"\] is an angle of \[5\] degrees, \[10\] minutes, and \[20\] seconds.

subject: Mathematics
q: By converting \[\frac{7\pi}{4} \] radians to degrees we get _____.
skill: Define a radian (measure of an angle in circular system) and prove the relationship between radians and degrees
feedback: \begin{align}\frac{7 \pi}{4} \mbox{ radians}&= \frac{7 \pi}{4} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \mbox{ degrees}\\\\&= 7 \times  45\\\\&= 315^\circ\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: Graphically show \[-\frac{3π}{5}\] radians in standard position.
skill: Define a radian (measure of an angle in circular system) and prove the relationship between radians and degrees
feedback:
Since \[-\frac{3π}{5}\] is negative, the terminal side must rotate in a clockwise direction; therefore, we can rule out the following graphs.





\begin{align}-\frac{3π}{5} &= -\frac{3 \times 180^\circ }{5}\\\\&= -\frac{540^\circ }{5}\\\\&=-108^\circ\end{align}

As \[-\frac{3π}{5} \mbox{ radians}= -108^\circ\], it lies between \[-90^\circ\] and \[-180^\circ\]. The following graph shows \[-\frac{3π}{5}\] radians in standard position.

subject: Mathematics
q: In the given circle, what is the length of the arc \[PQR=\]?
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the length of a circular arc l = rθ
feedback: \begin{align}\theta &= 360^\circ - 240^\circ\\&= 120^\circ \\ &= 120 \times \frac{\pi}{180} \mbox{ radians} \\\\ &= \frac{2 \pi}{3} \mbox{ radians} \\\\\mbox{Length of arc } PQR &= r \times \theta \\ &= 16 \times \frac{2 \pi}{3} \\\\&= \frac{32 \pi}{3} \mbox{ in}\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: An arc \[\overset{\huge\frown}{PQ}\] subtends an angle of \[60^\circ\] at the centre of a circle of radius \[2\] cm. What is the length of arc \[PQ\]?
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the length of a circular arc l = rθ
feedback: \begin{align}r&= 2\\\theta= 60^\circ&=\frac{\pi}{3} \mbox{ radians}\\\\\mbox{Length of a circular arc, } l&=r \theta\\\\l&= 2 \times \frac{\pi}{3}\\\\l&= \frac{2\pi}{3}\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: In a circle of radius \[5\] m, find the length of an arc which subtends an angle of \[54^\circ 27' 36''\] at the center of the circle.
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the length of a circular arc l = rθ
feedback:
First, we have to convert the angle into radians.

\begin{align}θ&=54^\circ 27' 36''\\\\θ&=54^\circ +\frac{27}{60}^\circ +\frac{36}{60×60}^\circ\\\\θ&=54^\circ +0.45^\circ +0.01^\circ\\\\θ&=54.46^\circ \\\\θ&=54.46×\frac{π}{180}\\\\θ& ≈0.9505 \mbox{ rad}\\\\l&=rθ\\l&=5×0.9505\\l&=4.7525\\l&≈4.75 \mbox{ m}\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: Let \[AB\] be an arc of length \[l\] subtending an angle \[θ\] at the centre of a circle of radius \[r\]. Which of the following statements is true?
options: \[θ\] varies directly as \[r\] and inversely as \[l\].
\[θ\] varies directly as \[l\] and inversely as \[r.\]
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the length of a circular arc l = rθ
feedback:
Recall that the formula for arc length is: \[l=rθ \]. This can be re-written as

\[θ=\frac{l}{r}\]

In this form, it is clear that \[θ\] varies directly as \[l\] and inversely as \[r\].

Note that the constant of proportionality is \[1\].

subject: Mathematics
q: What is the formula for the length \[l\] of an arc which subtends an angle \[θ\] degrees at the centre of a circle of radius \[r\]?
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the length of a circular arc l = rθ
feedback:
The formula for arc length is given as \[l=rθ\], where \[θ\] is in radians.
If \[θ\] is measured in degrees, we obtain

\[θ\] degrees\[ =θ \times \frac{π}{180}\] radians

Thus the formula is now expressed as \[l=rθ\frac{π}{180}\].

Important point:
Recall that the formula to convert degrees into radians is: \[x\] degrees \[=x\frac{π}{180}\] radians.

subject: Mathematics
q: The length \[l\] of an arc which subtends an angle \[θ\] radians at the centre of a circle of radius \[r\] is given as _____.
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the length of a circular arc l = rθ
feedback: \[l=rθ\] is the correct formula for arc length.

subject: Mathematics
q: A section of the circumference of a circle is called _____.
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the length of a circular arc l = rθ
feedback:
A section of the circumference of a circle is called an arc.
In a circle, arcs and chords are lines while sectors and segments are regions.

subject: Mathematics
q: Find the area of the sector of a circle with radius \[6\] cm and of angle \[60^\circ\].
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the area of a circular sector 1/2 r2θ
feedback:
\begin{align}r &= 6 \mbox{ cm} \\\theta &= 60^\circ \\&= 60^\circ \times \frac{\pi}{180} \mbox{ rad}\\\\&= \frac{\pi}{3} \mbox{ rad}\\\\\mbox{Area of sector} &= \frac{1}{2} r^2 \theta\\\\&= \frac{1}{2} \times 6^2 \times \frac{\pi}{3}\\\\&= \frac{1}{2} \times 36 \times \frac{22}{7 \times 3}\\\\&= 18 \times \frac{22}{21}\\\\&= \frac{396}{21}\\\\&= 18.86\mbox{ cm}^2\end{align}

Thus, the area of the sector of a circle with radius \[6\] cm and of angle \[60^\circ\] is \[18.86\] cm\[^2\].

subject: Mathematics
q: A sprinkler on a golf course fairway is set to spray water over a distance of \[70\] feet and rotates through an angle of \[120^\circ\], as shown in the diagram. Find the area that is watered by the sprinkler.
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the area of a circular sector 1/2 r2θ
feedback:
\begin{align}θ&=120^\circ\\\\&=120×\frac{π}{180}\\\\&=2.094 \mbox{ radians}\\\\\mbox{Area of the sector} &=\frac{1}{2} r^2 θ\\\\&=\frac{1}{2}(70)^2 (2.094) \\\\&=\frac{1}{2}(4900) (2.094)\\\\&=(2450) (2.094)\\\\&=5130 \; \mbox{ft}^2\end{align}

Therefore, the area watered by the sprinkler is \[5130 \mbox{ ft}^2\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Calculate the area of the sector shown in the image below.
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the area of a circular sector 1/2 r2θ
feedback:
\begin{align}θ&=30^\circ\\\\&=30×\frac{π}{180}\\\\&=0.5236 \mbox{ radians}\\\\\mbox{Area of the sector}&=\frac{1}{2} r^2 θ\\\\&=\frac{1}{2}(5)^2 (0.5236) \\\\&=\frac{1}{2}\times 25 \times (0.5236) \\\\&=12.5 \times (0.5236)\\\\&≈6.54\end{align}

Therefore, the area of the sector is \[6.54 \mbox{ m}^2\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider a circle of radius \[r\]. A sector with an arc length \[l\] subtends an angle \[θ\] degrees at the centre of the circle. The area of a sector is given by _____.
skill: To establish and apply the formula for the area of a circular sector 1/2 r2θ
feedback:
Recall that if the angle θ is in radian measure, the area of the sector is given by \[\frac{1}{2}r^2 θ\].

Since the angle \[θ\] is now measured in degrees, we have to convert it into radians.

\begin{align}θ \mbox{ degrees} &=\frac{π}{180} θ \mbox{ radians}\\\\\mbox{Area of the sector}&= \frac{1}{2}r^2 θ\\\\\mbox{Area of the sector}&=\frac{1}{2} r^2\frac{π}{180} θ\\\\\mbox{Area of the sector}&=\frac{π}{360} r^2 θ\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: Locate \[\frac{11}{19}π\] in standard position.
skill: Define and identify angle in standard position
feedback:
First, we convert \[\frac{11}{19}π\] to degrees:

\[\frac{11}{19} π=\frac{11}{19}×180^\circ ≈104^\circ \]

Since the obtained angle is positive, the following figures are eliminated.

        

Next, we see that \[104^\circ\] lies between \[0^\circ\] and \[180^\circ\]. Thus, the angle \[\frac{11}{19}π\] in standard position is given below.

subject: Mathematics
q: The area of a sector of a circle of radius \[3\] m is \[3π \; \mbox{m}^2\]. Find the angle \[θ\] subtended at the centre of the circle, and locate it in standard position.
skill: Define and identify angle in standard position
feedback:
Recall that the area \[A\] of a sector is given by

\[A=\frac{1}{2} r^2 θ\]

Since \[A=3π\] and \[r=3 \], we have

\begin{align}3π&=\frac{1}{2}(3^2)(θ)\\\\θ&=\frac{2π}{3}\; \mbox{radians}\\\\θ& =\left ( \frac{2π}{3} \right )\left ( \frac{180}{π} \right )\\\\θ&=120^\circ\end{align}

Since the angle is positive, the terminal side has to rotate anticlockwise. The resulting figure is given below.

subject: Mathematics
q: An angle \[θ\] is subtended at the centre of a circle of radius \[4\] m by an arc of length \[π\] m. Locate the angle in standard position.
skill: Define and identify angle in standard position
feedback:
Recall that arc length, \[l\], is related to the radius and angle as \[l=rθ \].

Since \[l=π\] m and \[r=4 \] m, we have

\begin{align}π&=4θ \\\\θ&=\frac{π}{4}\; \mbox{radians}\\θ& =\left ( \frac{π}{4} \right )\left ( \frac{180}{π} \right )\\\\θ&=45^\circ\end{align}

Since the angle is positive, the terminal side has to rotate anticlockwise. The resulting figure is given below.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Find the arithmetic mean of the following angles using the direct method. Then, draw the average angle in standard position.

\[-60.3^\circ, -165.7^\circ , -269^\circ \]
skill: Define and identify angle in standard position
feedback:
Using the direct method, we have

\begin{align}\mbox{Arithmetic mean} &=\frac{-60.3^\circ-165.7^\circ -269^\circ}{3}\\\\&=-\frac{495^\circ}{3}\\\\&=-165^\circ\end{align}

Since the angle is negative, the terminal side has to rotate clockwise. The figure is given below.

subject: Mathematics
q: Which of the following is not a quadrantal angle?
skill: Recognise quadrants and quadrantal angles
feedback:
Recall that quadrantal angles are the angles in standard position who have their terminal sides fall on either the \[x-\]axis or the \[y-\]axis. Therefore, they take values such as \[90^\circ, 180^\circ, 270^\circ, 360^\circ, 450^\circ,\] and so on. In other words, they are multiples of \[90^\circ\].
Of the four options, only \[330^\circ\] is not a multiple of \[90^\circ\], hence it is not a quadrantal angle.

subject: Mathematics
q: Which of the following statements is correct about quadrantal angles?
options: Quadrant angles are coterminal as well.
Quadrant angles do not have a standard position.
skill: Recognise quadrants and quadrantal angles
feedback:
Recall that quadrantal angles are angles in standard position with their terminal sides falling on either the \[x-\]axis or \[y-\]axis. Therefore, they take values such as \[90^\circ, 180^\circ, 270^\circ, 360^\circ, 450^\circ,\] and so on. In other words, they are multiples of \[90^\circ\]. Note that they can be negative as well, such as \[-90^\circ, -180^\circ,\] and\[-270^\circ\].
Quadrant angles are angles in standard position and some of them may also be multiples of \[180^\circ\]; for instance, \[180^\circ\] and \[360^\circ\].

subject: Mathematics
q: The quadrant in which an angle in standard position lies is determined by the location of its _____.
skill: Recognise quadrants and quadrantal angles
feedback:
The quadrant of an angle in standard position is measured by the position of the terminal side of the said angle.
Remember that the initial side of an angle in standard position always lies along the positive \[x-\]axis. The vertex of such angle is always at the origin, which is not part of any quadrant.

subject: Mathematics
q: A plane is divided into \[4\] _____ by the \[x-\]axis and the \[y-\]axis.
skill: Recognise quadrants and quadrantal angles
feedback:
Recall that the \[x\]-axis and the \[y\]-axis divide the plane into four regions called quadrants.
The origin is the point of intersection of the \[2\] axes while a sector is a part of a circle that is bounded by two radii and an arc.
A segment is a part of a circle that is bounded by an arc and a chord.

subject: Mathematics
q: The area of a sector of a circle of radius \[4\] m is \[5π \; \mbox{m}^2\], and it subtends an angle \[θ\] at the center of the circle. If \[θ\] is in standard position, identify the pair of quadrantal angles that encloses \[θ\].
skill: Recognise quadrants and quadrantal angles
feedback:
Recall the formula for the area of a sector:

\[A=\frac{1}{2} r^2 θ \]

Since \[A=5π\] and \[r=4\], we have

\begin{align}5π&=\frac{1}{2}(4^2)(θ)\\\\5π \times 2&=16 \times θ\\\\θ&= \frac{10π}{16}\\\\θ&=\frac{5π}{8}\; \mbox{ radians}\\\\θ&=\left ( \frac{5π}{8} \right )\left ( \frac{180}{π} \right )\\\\θ&=\frac{5 \times 180}{8}\\\\θ&=\frac{900}{8}\\\\θ&=112.5^\circ\end{align}

Thus the terminal side of \[θ\] lies in the second quadrant, which is bounded by quadrantal angles \[90^\circ\] and \[180^\circ\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Given a sector of a circle of radius \[5\] m and arc of length \[7π\] m, subtending an angle \[θ\] at the center of the circle. If \[θ\] is in standard position, identify the pair of quadrantal angles that encloses \[θ\].
skill: Recognise quadrants and quadrantal angles
feedback:
Recall the formula for arc length:

\[l=rθ\].

Since \[l=7π\] m and \[r=5\] m, we have

\begin{align}7π&=5θ \\\\θ&=\frac{7π}{5} \; \mbox{ radians}\\\\θ&=\left ( \frac{7π}{5} \right )\left ( \frac{180}{π} \right )\\\\θ&=\frac{7\times 180}{5}\\\\θ&=\frac{1260}{5}\\\\θ&=252^\circ\end{align}

Hence, the terminal side of \[θ\] lies in the third quadrant, which is bounded by quadrantal angles \[180^\circ\] and \[270^\circ\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Let \[α\] be an angle in standard position. Given that \[2\] is the fourth proportional of \[α\], \[36^\circ\], and \[7\], we can find \[α\] in the _____ quadrant.
skill: Recognise quadrants and quadrantal angles
feedback:
Since \[2\] is the fourth proportional of \[α\], \[36^\circ\], and \[7\], we have

\begin{align}α:36&=7:2 \\\mbox{Product of extremes}&= \mbox{Product of means}\\2(α)&=7(36) \\2(α)&=252 \\\\α&=\frac{252}{2} \\\\∴α&=126^\circ\end{align}

As \[α=126^\circ\], the terminal side of \[α\] lies in the second quadrant.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let \[α\] and \[β\] be two angles in standard position. If \[90^\circ< α< 180^\circ\] and \[β\] is coterminal with \[α\], we can find the terminal side of \[β\] in the _____ quadrant.
skill: Recognise quadrants and quadrantal angles
feedback:
Since \[β\] is coterminal with \[α\], the terminal sides of \[α\] and \[β\] must coincide. Since \[90^\circ< α< 180^\circ\], the terminal side of \[α\] lies in the second quadrant.
Therefore, the terminal side of \[β\] must also lie in the second quadrant.

subject: Mathematics
q: In the right-angled triangle given below, the length of the line segment \[AB\] is \[13\] units and the length of the line segment \[CB\] is \[12\] units. What is the tangent of angle \[A\]?
skill: Define trigonometric ratios and their reciprocals with the help of a unit circle
feedback: \begin{align}\mbox{Hypotenuse}^2 &= \mbox{Perpendicular}^2 + \mbox{Base}^2\\(m\overline{AB})^2 &= (m\overline{AC})^2 + (m\overline{CB})^2\\13^2 &= (m\overline{AC})^2 + 12^2 \\(m\overline{AC})^2&= 13^2-12^2\\(m\overline{AC})^2&= 169-144\\(m\overline{AC})^2&= 25\\m\overline{AC}&=5\\\\\tan A &= \frac{\mbox{Perpendicular}}{\mbox{Base}} \\\\\tan A &= \frac{m\overline{CB}}{m\overline{AC}} \\\\\tan A &= \frac{12}{5}\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: Find the value of \[cot \;θ\] in the following diagram (not drawn to scale).
skill: Define trigonometric ratios and their reciprocals with the help of a unit circle
feedback:
\[tan(θ) =\frac{AC}{BC}\]

\[cot (θ)=\frac{1}{tan(θ)}\]

\[cot (θ) =\frac{BC}{AC}\]

\[cot (θ)=\frac{3}{4}\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Find the exact value of \[tan (θ)\] based on the following diagram (not drawn to scale).
skill: Define trigonometric ratios and their reciprocals with the help of a unit circle
feedback:
In this diagram, \[tan (θ)=\frac{BC}{AB}\].

Thus, we will find \[AB\] by using Pythagoras' theorem:

\begin{align}(AB)^2+(BC)^2&=(AC)^2 \\\\AB&=\sqrt{(AC)^2-(BC)^2}\\\\AB&=\sqrt{13^2-9^2}\\\\AB&=\sqrt{169-81}\\\\AB&=\sqrt{88}\\\\AB&=2\sqrt{22} \\\\tan (θ)&=\frac{BC}{AB}\\\\tan (θ)&=\frac{9}{2\sqrt{22}}\end{align}.

subject: Mathematics
q: The trigonometric functions can be defined by using  _____.
skill: Define trigonometric ratios and their reciprocals with the help of a unit circle
feedback: In order to define the trigonometric functions, we use a unit circle (a circle with a radius equal to \[1\] unit). The diagram of the circle is given below.

subject: Mathematics
q: Based on the following diagram, what trigonometric functions can be derived?
skill: Define trigonometric ratios and their reciprocals with the help of a unit circle
feedback:
From the unit circle, we can obtain the following:

\begin{align}sin (\phi)&=\frac{EP}{OP}\\\\sin (\phi)&=\frac{y}{1}\\\\sin (\phi)&=y \\\\cos (\phi)&=\frac{OE}{OP}\\\\cos (\phi)&=\frac{x}{1}\\\\cos (\phi)&=x\end{align}

An important point to remember:

The sine and the cosine should be as follows:

\[sin (\phi)=\frac{\mbox{opposite side}}{\mbox{hypotenuse}}\]

\[cos (\phi )=\frac{\mbox{adjacent side}}{\mbox{hypotenuse}}\]

subject: Mathematics
q: Based on the given figure, \[sin (\phi) + cos (\phi) =\]_____.
skill: Define trigonometric ratios and their reciprocals with the help of a unit circle
feedback:
Based on the diagram, we have;

\begin{align}sin (\phi)&=\frac{EP}{OP}\\\\sin (\phi)&=\frac{y}{1}\\\\sin (\phi)&=y \\\\cos (\phi)&=\frac{OE}{OP}\\\\cos (\phi)&=\frac{x}{1}\\\\cos (\phi)&=x\end{align}

Therefore, \[sin (\phi) + cos (\phi) = y+x\].

An important point to remember:

The sine and the cosine are as follows:

\[sin (\phi )=\frac{\mbox{opposite side}}{\mbox{hypotenuse}}\]

\[cos (\phi )=\frac{\mbox{adjacent side}}{\mbox{hypotenuse}}\]

subject: Mathematics
q: \[cosec (θ) =\] _____.
skill: Define trigonometric ratios and their reciprocals with the help of a unit circle
feedback: Recall that the cosecant is the reciprocal of the sine. Therefore, \[cosec (θ)=\frac{1}{sin(θ)}\].

subject: Mathematics
q: If \[\sec \theta = 2\], then \[\theta\] will be _____.
skill: Recall the values of trigonometric ratios for 45°, 30° and 60°
feedback: \begin{align}\sec \theta &= 2\\\\\frac{1}{\cos \ \theta}&=2\\\\\cos \theta &=\frac{1}{2}\\\\\theta &= \cos^{-1}\frac{1}{2}\\\\\theta &= 60^\circ\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: If \[x=45^\circ\], then find the value of \[(1-\cos^{2}x-\sin^{2}x)\].
skill: Recall the values of trigonometric ratios for 45°, 30° and 60°

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider the right triangle \[ABC\] below. The sides opposite to the vertices \[A\] and \[B\] are denoted by \[a\] and \[b\] respectively. Given that \[\sqrt{3} b\] is the mean proportional of \[a\] and \[4a\], find \[θ\].
skill: Recall the values of trigonometric ratios for 45°, 30° and 60°
feedback:
Given that \[\sqrt{3} b\] is the mean proportional of \[a\] and \[4a\], we have

\begin{align}a:\sqrt{3} b&=\sqrt{3} b:4a \;\;\;\;\;(\because \mbox{Product of extremes } = \mbox{ Product of means})\\\\a \times 4a &=\sqrt{3} b \times \sqrt{3} b\\\\4a^2&=3b^2 \\\\\frac{a^2}{b^2}&=\frac{3}{4}\\\\\frac{a}{b}&=\frac{\sqrt{3}}2{}\\\\\mbox{From the diagram,}& \mbox{ we have}\\\\cos\; θ&=\frac{a}{b}\\\\⇒cos \; θ&=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\\\\cos \; 30^\circ&=\frac{\sqrt{3}}2{}\\\\⇒θ&=30^\circ\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider the right triangle \[ABC\] below. The sides opposite to the vertices \[A\] and \[B\] are denoted by \[a\] and \[b\] respectively. Given that \[4a\] is the third proportional of \[a\] and \[2b\], find \[θ.\]
skill: Recall the values of trigonometric ratios for 45°, 30° and 60°
feedback:
Given that \[4a\] is the third proportional of \[a\] and \[2b\], we have

\begin{align}a:2b&=2b:4a \;\;\;\;\;(\because \mbox{Product of extremes } = \mbox{ Product of means})\\\\a \times 4a &= 2b \times 2b \\\\4a^2&=4b^2 \\\\\frac{a^2}{b^2}&=1\\\\\frac{a}{b}&=1 \\\\\mbox{From the diagram,}& \mbox{ we have}\\\\tan\; θ&=\frac{a}{b}\\\\⇒tan \; θ&=1 \\\\\mbox{We know that } tan \; 45^\circ&=1 \\\\⇒θ&=45^\circ\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider the right triangle \[ABC\] below. The sides opposite to the vertices \[A\] and \[B\] are denoted by \[a\] and \[b\] respectively. Given that \[a:1=b:\sqrt{2}\], find \[θ\].
skill: Recall the values of trigonometric ratios for 45°, 30° and 60°
feedback:
Recall that in a proportion, the product of extremes \[=\] the product of means. Thus, from \[a:1=b:\sqrt{2}\], we get

\begin{align}(a)(\sqrt{2})&=(1)(b) \\\\\frac{a}{b}&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\\\\\mbox{From the diagram,}& \mbox{ we have}\\\\sin \; θ &= \frac{a}{b}\\\\⇒ sin \; θ &=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\\\\\mbox{We know that } sin\; 45^\circ &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\\\\⇒ θ&=45^\circ\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: If \[\sin \theta<0\] and \[\tan \theta>0\], then \[\theta\] will be in the _____ quadrant.
skill: Recognise signs of trigonomotric ratios in different quadrants
feedback:
\[\sin \theta\] is negative in the \[3^{\text{rd}}\] and \[4^{\text{th}}\] quadrants.
\[\tan \theta \] is positive in the \[1^{\text{st}}\] and \[3^{\text{rd}}\] quadrants.
Therefore, if \[\sin \theta<0\] and \[\tan \theta>0\], then \[\theta\] will be in the \[3^{\text{rd}}\] quadrant.

subject: Mathematics
q: If \[\sec \theta < 0\] and \[\sin \theta < 0\], then \[\theta\] lies in which quadrant?
skill: Recognise signs of trigonomotric ratios in different quadrants
feedback:
For any value of \[\theta\], \[\sin <0\] in quadrants III and IV.
For any value of \[\theta\], \[\sec <0\] in quadrants II and III.
Quadrant III is the quadrant where \[\sec<0\] and \[\sin <0\].

subject: Mathematics
q: In which quadrant are all trigonometric ratios positive?
skill: Recognise signs of trigonomotric ratios in different quadrants
feedback: In the first quadrant, both coordinates of any point are positive. So no matter what ratios we take, they will always be positive.

subject: Mathematics
q: The expression \[r = \sqrt{x^2+ y^2}\] _____.
options: may be either positive or negative
skill: Recognise signs of trigonomotric ratios in different quadrants
feedback: Recall that \[r =\sqrt{x^2+ y^2}\] represents the hypotenuse of the triangle, or in other words, the distance of the point \[(x,y)\] from the origin. Hence, it is always positive. It cannot be \[0\] because whenever an angle is being subtended at the origin, the terminal side of the angle must be present, resulting in a non-zero hypotenuse.

subject: Mathematics
q: The area of a sector of a circle of radius \[3\]m is given by \[7π \mbox{ m}^2\]. The angle subtended at the centre of the circle by this sector is \[θ\]. Find the signs of the trigonometric ratios \[sin \; θ,\; sec \; θ,\; and\; cot \; θ\].
skill: Recognise signs of trigonomotric ratios in different quadrants
feedback:
Recall the formula for the area \[A\] of a sector:

\[A=\frac{1}{2} r^2 θ \]

Since \[A=7π \mbox{ m}^2\] and \[r=3\] m, we have

\[7π=\frac{1}{2} (3^2)(θ) \]

\[θ=\frac{14π}{9} \mbox{ radians}\]

\[θ=\left ( \frac{14π}{9} \right )\left ( \frac{180}{π} \right )\]

\[θ=280^\circ \]

Since \[θ\] lies in the fourth quadrant, \[sin \; θ < 0\], \[cos \; θ > 0⇒ sec \; θ> 0 \] and \[tan \; θ< 0⇒cot \; θ< 0\].

subject: Mathematics
q: The angle \[θ\] is subtended at the centre of a circle of radius \[4\] m by an arc of length \[5π \] m. Find the signs of the trigonometric ratios \[sec\; θ\] and \[cosec\; θ\].
skill: Recognise signs of trigonomotric ratios in different quadrants
feedback:
Recall how the arc length \[l\] is related to the radius \[r\] as:

\[l=rθ \]

Since \[l=5π\] m and \[r=4\] m, we have

\begin{align}5π&=4θ \\\\θ&=\frac{5π}{4} \mbox{ radians}\\\\θ&=\left ( \frac{5π}{4} \right )\left ( \frac{180}{π} \right )\\\\θ&=225^\circ \end{align}

Since \[θ\] lies in the third quadrant, we have \[cos \; θ< 0\] and \[sin \; θ< 0\].

Therefore, \[sec \; θ=\frac{1}{cos \;θ} < 0\] and \[cosec \; θ=\frac{1}{sin\; θ}< 0\].

subject: Mathematics
q: If \[θ:90^\circ=3:2\], find the signs of \[sin \; θ,\; cos \; θ,\] and \[tan \; θ\].
skill: Recognise signs of trigonomotric ratios in different quadrants
feedback:
\begin{align}θ:90^\circ&=3:2\\\mbox{Product of extremes }&= \mbox{ Product of means}\\(2)(θ)&=(3)(90) \\2θ&=270\\θ&=135^\circ \end{align}

Since \[θ\] lies in the second quadrant. Therefore, \[sin\;θ> 0,\; cos\;θ< 0,\] and \[tan\;θ< 0\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Let \[sin\;θ\] and \[cos\;θ\] be solutions of the quadratic equation \[x^2-Sx+P=0\]. If \[S> 0\] and \[P> 0\], find the quadrant of \[θ\].
skill: Recognise signs of trigonomotric ratios in different quadrants
feedback:
Lets denote \[sin\;θ\] by \[s\] and \[cos\;θ\] by \[c\].
Since \[s\] and \[c\] are solutions of the quadratic equation \[x^2-Sx+P=0\], we have:
Sum of roots, \[S=s+c \]
Product of roots, \[P=sc\]
Since \[P=sc> 0\], \[s\] and \[c\] must have the same sign and as \[S=s+c> 0\], both \[s\] and \[c\] must be positive.
The only quadrant where \[sin\;θ> 0\] and \[cos\;θ> 0\] is the first quadrant.

subject: Mathematics
q: In the given figure, \[\sin\ x = \frac{5}{13}\]. Find the values of \[\cos\ x\] and \[\tan\ x\].
skill: Find values of remaining trigonometric ratios if one trigonometric ratio is given

subject: Mathematics
q: Find the values of \[cosec\] and \[tan\] at \[θ = 0^\circ\].
skill: Calculate the values of trigonometric ratios for 0°, 90°, 180°, 270° and 360°
feedback:
\[cosec \; 0^\circ = ∞ ,\; tan \; 0^\circ = 0\].
If you are unable to recall this information, always go back to how the values are derived. For \[0^\circ\], we consider point \[P(1,0),\] which is along the positive \[x-axis\] as follows.



Here, \[x = 1\] and \[y = 0\], and \[r = 1\].
Therefore,

\begin{align}cosec \; 0^\circ&=\frac{1}{sin \; 0^\circ}\\\\& =\frac{1}{\left ( \frac{y}{r} \right )}\\\\&=\frac{r}{y}\\\\&=\frac{1}{0}\\\\&=∞\\\\tan \; 0^\circ&=\frac{y}{x}\\\\&=\frac{0}{1}\\\\&=0\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: \[\text{cosec}^{2} \theta- \cot^{2}\theta=\] _____
skill: Prove the trigonometric identities and apply them to show different trigonometric relations
feedback: \begin{align}\text{cosec}^{2} \theta- \cot^{2}\theta &=\frac{1}{\sin^2 \theta} - \frac{\cos^2\theta}{\sin^2 \theta}\\\\&=\frac{1-\cos^2 \theta}{\sin^2\theta}\\\\&=\frac{\sin^2 \theta}{\sin^2\theta} \;\;\;\;\;  (\because 1-\cos^2\theta = \sin^2\theta)\\\\&=1\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: If \[1+\tan^{2}45=\sec^{2}\theta\], then \[\theta=\] _____.
skill: Prove the trigonometric identities and apply them to show different trigonometric relations
feedback: \begin{align}1+\tan^{2}45&=\sec^{2}\theta\\1+(1)^2&=\sec^2\theta\\2&=\sec^2\theta\\\sqrt{2}&=\sec \ \theta\\\theta&= \sec^{-1} \sqrt{2}\\\theta&= 45^\circ\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: \[\frac{cosec \ θ-sec  \ θ}{cosec \ θ+sec \ θ}=\] _____.
skill: Prove the trigonometric identities and apply them to show different trigonometric relations
feedback:
\[\frac{cosec \ θ-sec \ θ}{cosec \ θ+sec \ θ}\]

Dividing the numerator and the denominator by \[cosec \ θ \], we get:

\begin{align}\frac{cosec \ θ-sec \ θ}{cosec \ θ+sec \ θ}&=\left ( \frac{\frac{cosec \ θ}{cosec \ θ}-\frac{sec \ θ}{cosec \ θ}}{\frac{cosec \ θ}{cosec \ θ}+\frac{sec \ θ}{cosec \ θ}} \right ) \\\\\frac{cosec \ θ-sec \ θ}{cosec \ θ+sec \ θ}&=\frac{1-\frac{sec \ θ}{cosec \ θ}}{1+\frac{sec \ θ}{cosec \ θ}}\\\\\frac{sec \ θ}{cosec \ θ}&=\frac{\frac{1}{cos \ θ}}{\frac{1}{sin \ θ}}\\\\\frac{sec \ θ}{cosec \ θ}&=\frac{sin \ θ}{cos \ θ}\\\\\frac{1-\frac{sin \ θ}{cos \ θ}}{1+\frac{sin \ θ}{cos \ θ}}&=\frac{1-tan \ θ}{1+tan \ θ}\end{align}

Therefore, \[\frac{cosec \ θ-sec \ θ}{cosec \ θ+sec \ θ}= \frac{1-tan \ θ}{1+tan \ θ}\].

subject: Mathematics
q: \[tan \ θ+cot \ θ=\] _____.
skill: Prove the trigonometric identities and apply them to show different trigonometric relations
feedback:
\begin{align}tan \ θ+cot \ θ &=\frac{sin \ θ}{cos \ θ}+\frac{cos \ θ}{sin \ θ}\\\\&=\frac{sin \ θ\; sin \ θ+cos \ θ\; cos \ θ}{cos \ θ \; sin \ θ}\\\\&=\frac{sin^2 \ θ+cos^2 \ θ}{cos \ θ\; sin \ θ}\\\\&=\frac{1}{cos \ θ \; sin \ θ}\\\\&=\frac{1}{cos \ θ} \; \frac{1}{sin \ θ}\\\\& =sec \ θ \; cosec \ θ\end{align}

Therefore, \[tan \ θ+cot \ θ=sec \ θ \; cosec \ θ\].

subject: Mathematics
q: \[\frac{sin \ θ}{1-cos \ θ}=\] _____.
skill: Prove the trigonometric identities and apply them to show different trigonometric relations
feedback:
\begin{align}\frac{sin \ θ}{1-cos \ θ}&=\left ( \frac{sin \ θ}{1-cos \ θ} \right )×\left ( \frac{1+cos \ θ}{1+cos \ θ} \right ) \\\\&=\frac{(sin \ θ)(1+cos \ θ)}{(1-cos \ θ)(1+cos \ θ)}\\\\&=\frac{sin \ θ(1+cos \ θ)}{1-cos^2 \ θ}\\\\&=\frac{sin \ θ(1+cos \ θ)}{sin^2 \ θ}\\\\&=\frac{1+cos \ θ}{sin \ θ}\end{align}

Therefore, \[\frac{sin \ θ}{1-cos \ θ}=\frac{1+cos \ θ}{sin \ θ}\].

subject: Mathematics
q: \[\frac{1-sin \ θ}{cos \ θ}=\] _____.
skill: Prove the trigonometric identities and apply them to show different trigonometric relations
feedback:
\begin{align}\frac{1-sin \ θ}{cos \ θ}&=\left ( \frac{1-sin \ θ}{cos \ θ} \right )×\left ( \frac{1+sin \ θ}{1+sin \ θ} \right )\\\\&=\frac{(1-sin \ θ)(1+sin \ θ)}{cos \ θ(1+sin \ θ)}\\\\&=\frac{1-sin^2 \ θ}{cos \ θ(1+sin \ θ)}\\\\&=\frac{cos^2 \ θ}{cos \ θ(1+sin \ θ)}\\\\&=\frac{cos \ θ}{1+sin \ θ}\end{align}

Therefore, \[\frac{1-sin \ θ}{cos \ θ}=\frac{cos \ θ}{1+sin \ θ}\].

subject: Mathematics
q: If the ratio of the height of a tower and the length of its shadow is \[\sqrt{3}:1\], what is the angle of elevation of shadow?

feedback: \begin{align}\mbox{Height of the tower} &= \mbox{Perpendicular} \\\mbox{Shadow of the tower } &= \mbox{Base} \\\\\frac{\mbox{Perpendicular}}{\mbox{Base}} &= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{1}\\\\\tan \theta &= \sqrt 3\\\theta &= \tan^{-1} \ \sqrt{3}\\\theta &= 60^\circ\end{align}

feedback:

subject: Mathematics
q: For this triangle, find \[h\] given that \[a = 12\] cm, \[b = 6.5\] cm, and \[c = 8.5\] cm.
topic: Projection of a Side of a Triangle
skill: Prove and apply triangle theorem 1: (BC)^2 = (AC)^2 + (AB)^2 + 2(AB)(AD)
feedback:
\begin{align}a^2&=b^2+c^2+2cd \\\\d&=\frac{a^2-b^2-c^2}{2c}\\\\&=\frac{12^2-6.5^2-8.5^2}{2(8.5)}\\\\& ≈1.7353\end{align}

With this value, we can then calculate \[h\] by applying Pythagoras theorem to triangle \[ABD\].

\begin{align}b^2&=h^2+d^2\\h&=\sqrt{b^2-d^2}\\&=\sqrt{6.5^2-1.7353^2 }\\& ≈6.26 cm\end{align}

Therefore, \[h= 6.26\] cm.

subject: Mathematics
q:
In \[?ABC\], m\[\overline{AB}=4\] cm, m\[\overline{AC}=13\] cm, and m\[∠BAC=150^0\]. Find m\[\overline{BC}\].
Hint: you need to consider the obtuse angle at \[A\] in your solution.
topic: Projection of a Side of a Triangle
skill: Prove and apply triangle theorem 1: (BC)^2 = (AC)^2 + (AB)^2 + 2(AB)(AD)
feedback:
Based on the given information, we can draw the following triangle:


\begin{align}(BC)^2 &= (AC)^2 + (AB)^2 + 2(AB)(AD)\\\mbox{Applying this theorem to } & Δ ABC\mbox{, we get}\\a^2&=13^2+4^2+2(4)(AD) --- (1)\\\mbox{To get } AD & \mbox{, we need to consider } ∠CAD\\\\cos∠CAD &=\frac{AD}{AC}\\\\⇒cos(180^0-150^0 )&=\frac{AD}{13} \\\\⇒AD&=13 \times cos30^0& \\\\AD&=\frac{13\sqrt{3}}{2}\\\\\mbox{Substituting this value} & \mbox{ into equation (1)}\\a^2&=13^2+4^2+2(4)\frac{13\sqrt{3}}{2}) \\\\a & ≈16.6\end{align}

Therefore, m\[\overline{BC}=16.6\] cm.

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider the triangle \[ABC\], and the right triangle \[BCD\] as shown in the diagram. Given that \[∠CAB\] is obtuse, express \[\frac{a}{b}\] in terms of \[θ\].
topic: Projection of a Side of a Triangle
skill: Prove and apply triangle theorem 1: (BC)^2 = (AC)^2 + (AB)^2 + 2(AB)(AD)
feedback:
Since \[m \overline{(CA)}=m\overline{(AB)}\] we have
\[(m\overline{BC})^2=2(m\overline{AB})(m\overline{BD}) \]

Dividing both sides by \[(m\overline{BC})(m\overline{AB})\], we get

\[\frac{m\overline{BC}}{m\overline{AB}}=2\frac{m\overline{BD}}{m\overline{BC}}----- (1) \]

From the diagram, we have

\[cosθ=\frac{m\overline{BD}}{m\overline{BC}} ----- (2) \]

Substituting equation (2) into equation (1), and using \[m\overline{BC}=a\] and \[m\overline{AB}=b\], we finally obtain
\[\frac{a}{b}=\frac{m\overline{BC}}{m\overline{AB}}=2cosθ\]

subject: Mathematics
q: The triangle \[ABC\] and the right triangle \[BCD\] are drawn in a circle of radius \[b\] as shown in the diagram. Given that \[θ\] is an obtuse angle, express \[a^2\] in terms of \[b\], \[c\] and \[cosθ\].
topic: Projection of a Side of a Triangle
skill: Prove and apply triangle theorem 1: (BC)^2 = (AC)^2 + (AB)^2 + 2(AB)(AD)
feedback:
Note that \[\overline{(AC)}\] is the terminal side of angle \[θ\]. Since \[θ\] lies in the second quadrant, that means \[cosθ< 0\].

\begin{align}⇒cosθ &=-\frac{x}{b} \mbox{-----(1)} \\\\\mbox{We know that}&\\a^2&=b^2+c^2+2cx \mbox{-----(2)}\\\mbox{From equation }&1 \mbox{, we get}\\x&=-bcosθ \mbox{-----(3)}\\\mbox{Substituting equation } &3 \mbox{ into equation } 2 \mbox{, we obtain}\\a^2&=b^2+c^2-2bc(cosθ)\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider the triangle \[ABC\], and the right triangle \[BCD\] shown in the diagram. Given that \[∠CAB\] is obtuse, find \[a^2\].
topic: Projection of a Side of a Triangle
skill: Prove and apply triangle theorem 1: (BC)^2 = (AC)^2 + (AB)^2 + 2(AB)(AD)
feedback:
Since \[ACD\] is a right triangle, by the Pythagorean theorem we have
\[b^2=h^2+x^2 ----- (1) \]
Since \[BCD\] is also a right triangle, by the Pythagorean theorem we have
\[a^2=h^2+(x+c)^2 \]
\[a^2=h^2+x^2+2xc+c^2 --- (2) \]
Substituting equation \[(1)\] into equation \[(2)\], we have
\[a^2=b^2+2xc+c^2 \]
This is, in fact, the derivation of the theorem \[1\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider the two line segments \[\overline{XY}\] and \[\overline{XZ}\] shown in the diagram below. Which point represents the projection of point \[Y\] on \[\overline{XZ}\]?
topic: Projection of a Side of a Triangle
skill: Prove and apply triangle theorem 1: (BC)^2 = (AC)^2 + (AB)^2 + 2(AB)(AD)
feedback:
To find the projection of a point on a line segment, we have to draw a line from that point in a way that it intersects the line segment perpendicularly. The point of intersection is then the projection of that point on the line segment.
It may be helpful to visualise this way: If we shine a torch vertically above point \[Y\], the shadow of point \[Y\] (i.e. its projection) will fall on point \[C\].

subject: Mathematics
q: What is an obtuse angle?
options: An angle which is more than \[0^0\] but less than \[90^0\].
An angle which is more than \[90^0\] but less than \[180^0\].
An angle which is more than \[180^0\] but less than \[270^0\].
An angle which is more than \[270^0\] but less than \[360^0\].
topic: Projection of a Side of a Triangle
skill: Prove and apply triangle theorem 1: (BC)^2 = (AC)^2 + (AB)^2 + 2(AB)(AD)
feedback:
Recall that an obtuse angle is one which is more than \[90^0\] but less than \[180^0\].
The measurement of an acute angle ranges between \[0^0\] and \[90^0\].
Angles between \[180^0\] and \[360^0\] are called reflex angles.

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider the triangle \[ABC\] as shown in the diagram, with dimensions in cm. Given that \[∠CAB\] is an acute angle, find the value of \[b\] if \[tanθ=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3}\].
topic: Projection of a Side of a Triangle
skill: Prove and apply triangle theorem 2: (BC)^2 = (AC)^2 + (AB)^2 - 2(AB)(AD)
feedback:
Denoting \[m\overline{AD}\] by \[x\] and applying theorem 2 on the given triangle, we get:

\begin{align}\left ( \sqrt{\frac{7}{2}} \right )^2&=b^2+b^2-2bx \\\\\frac{7}{2}&=2b^2-2bx\\\\\frac{7}{2}&=2b(b-x) \mbox{--- (1)}\\\mbox{From the diagram,}& \mbox{we have:}\\cosθ &=\frac{b-x}{\sqrt{\frac{7}{2}}}\\\\b-x&=\sqrt{\frac{7}{2}} cosθ \mbox{--- (2)}\\\\\mbox{Substituting equation (2)} &\mbox{into equation (1), we have:} \\\\\frac{7}{2}&=\sqrt{14}\: bcosθ \\\\b&=\frac{\sqrt{7}}{2\sqrt{2} cosθ} \mbox{--- (3)}\end{align}

We are given that \[tanθ=\frac{\sqrt{5}}3{}\]. Recall how we can obtain other trigonometric ratios from one known ratio.

In the right-angled triangle \[LMN,\]

\begin{align}tanθ&=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3}=\frac{l}{m}⇒ \\\\l&=\sqrt{5}\\m&=3 \\\\\mbox{Using Pythagorean theorem: }\\\\(LM)^2=l^2+m^2 &=(\sqrt{5})^2+3^2\\⇒(LM)^2 &=5+9=14 \\⇒LM &=\sqrt{14}\\cosθ &=\frac{m}{LM}\\\\cosθ &=\frac{3}{\sqrt{14}} \mbox{--- (4)} \\\mbox{Substituting equation (4)} &\mbox{into equation (3), we have:}\\\\b&=\frac{\sqrt{7}}{2(\sqrt{2})\frac{3}{\sqrt{14}}}\\\\&= \frac{\sqrt{7}}{\left ( \frac{6}{\sqrt{7}} \right )}\\\\b&=\frac{7}{6}\end{align}

Therefore, \[b=\frac{7}{6}\] cm.

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider the triangle \[ABC\] as shown in the diagram, with dimensions in cm. Given that \[θ\] is an acute angle, find the value of \[a\] if \[tanθ=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}.\]
topic: Projection of a Side of a Triangle
skill: Prove and apply triangle theorem 2: (BC)^2 = (AC)^2 + (AB)^2 - 2(AB)(AD)
feedback:
Denoting \[m\overline{AD}\] by \[x\] and applying theorem \[2\] on the given triangle, we get:
\begin{align}a^2&=(\sqrt{28})^2+5^2-2(5)(x)\\a^2&=28+25-10x\\a^2&=53-10x \mbox{--- (1)}\\\\\mbox{From the diagram,}&\mbox{we have:}\\\\cosθ&=\frac{x}{\sqrt{28}}\\\\x&=\sqrt{28} cosθ \mbox{--- (2)}\\\\\mbox{Substituting equation (2)} &\mbox{into equation (1), we have"} \\\\a^2&=53-10\sqrt{28} cosθ \mbox{--- (3)}\end{align}

We are given that \[tanθ=\frac{\sqrt{3}}2{}\]. Recall how we can obtain other trigonometric ratios from one known ratio.

In the right-angled triangle \[LMN\],

\begin{align}tanθ&=\frac{3}{2}\\\\&=\frac{l}{m}\\\\l&=\sqrt{3}\\m&=2 \\\\\mbox{Using Pythagorean theorem:}\\\\(LM)^2&=l^2+m^2\\&=(\sqrt{3})^2+2^2\\⇒(LM)^2&=3+4\\&=7\\⇒LM &=\sqrt{7}\\\\cosθ &=\frac{m}{LM}\\\\cosθ&=\frac{2}{\sqrt{7}} \mbox{--- (4)}\\\\\mbox{Substituting equation (4)}& \mbox{into equation (3), we have:}\\\\a^2&=53-10\sqrt{28}\left ( \frac{2}{\sqrt{2}} \right ) \\\\a^2&=53-40\\&=13\end{align}

Therefore, \[a=\sqrt{13 }\] cm.

subject: Mathematics
q: In the triangle below, find the length of \[AD\].
topic: Projection of a Side of a Triangle
skill: Prove and apply triangle theorem 2: (BC)^2 = (AC)^2 + (AB)^2 - 2(AB)(AD)
feedback:
Applying theorem \[2\], we get the equation:

\begin{align}6^2&=8^2+10^2-2(10)(BD)\\\\BD&=\frac{8^2+10^2-6^2}{2(10)}\\\\&=\frac{64+100-36}{20}\\\\&=\frac{128}{20}\\\\&=6.4\\\\\mbox{To get}AD \mbox{, we apply the}&\mbox{Pythagorean theorem to} ΔABC\\\\8^2&=(AD)^2+(BD)^2\\AD&=\sqrt{8^2-(BD)^2}\\&=\sqrt{64-6.4^2}\\&=4.8\end{align}

Therefore, \[AD=4.8\].

subject: Mathematics
q:
Bob tried to prove the theorem \[(BC)^2=(AC)^2+(AB)^2-2(m\overline{AB})(m\overline{AD})\] based on the triangle below, but he made a mistake. In which line of his working, shown below, is the mistake?


In \[∠rt ΔACD: \]
Line \[1\]: \[(AC)^2=(AD)^2+(CD)^2 \]
Line \[2\]: \[b^2=x^2+h^2 \]
In \[∠rt ΔBCD: \]
Line \[3\]: \[(BC)^2=(BD)^2+(CD)^2 \]
Line \[4\]: \[a^2=(c-x)^2+h^2 \]
Line \[5\]: \[a^2=c^2-2cx+x^2+h^2 \]
Line \[6\]: \[a^2=c^2-2cx-b^2 \]
Line \[7\]: \[a^2=b^2+c^2-2cx\]
Therefore,
Line \[8\]: \[(BC)^2=(AC)^2+(AB)^2+2(m\overline{AB})(m\overline{AD})\]
topic: Projection of a Side of a Triangle
skill: Prove and apply triangle theorem 2: (BC)^2 = (AC)^2 + (AB)^2 - 2(AB)(AD)
feedback: The substitution in line \[6\] is wrong. It should be \[a^2=c^2-2cx+b^2 \] instead of \[a^2=c^2-2cx-b^2 \].

subject: Mathematics
q: In the triangle below, find m\[\overline{AC}\].
topic: Projection of a Side of a Triangle
skill: Prove and apply triangle theorem 2: (BC)^2 = (AC)^2 + (AB)^2 - 2(AB)(AD)
feedback:
Using theorem \[2\], we get the equation:

\begin{align}a^2&=8^2+6^2-2(8)(BD)\\\\\mbox{To find} & BD\mbox{, we consider: } ∠ABC\\\\cos ∠ABC&=\frac{BD}{AB}\\\\⇒cos60^0 &=\frac{BD}{6} \\\\BD&=6 \; cos60^0\\&=3 \\\\\mbox{Substituting this into} &\mbox{ the first equation, we get:}\\\\a^2&=8^2+6^2-2(8)(3)\\&=64+36-48\\&=100-58\\&=52\\\\a&=\sqrt{52}\\a&=2\sqrt{13}\end{align}

Therefore, m\[\overline{AC}=2\sqrt{13}\].

subject: Mathematics
q:
The following is an incorrect proof of the theorem \[(BC)^2=(AC)^2+(AB)^2-2(m\overline{AB})(m\overline{AD})\]. Which steps need to be switched?


In \[∠rt \; ΔACD: \]
Step \[1\]: \[(AC)^2=(AD)^2+(CD)^2 \]
Step \[2\]: \[b^2=x^2+h^2 \]
In \[∠rt \; ΔCBD: \]
Step \[3\]: \[(BC)^2=(BD)^2+(CD)^2 \]
Step \[4\]: \[a^2=c^2-2cx+b^2 \]
Step \[5\]: \[a^2=c^2-2cx+x^2+h^2 \]
Step \[6\]: \[a^2=(c-x)^2+h^2 \]
Step \[7\]: \[a^2=b^2+c^2-2cx\]
Therefore,
Step \[8\]: \[(BC)^2=(AC)^2+(AB)^2-2(m\overline{AB})(m\overline{AD})\]
topic: Projection of a Side of a Triangle
skill: Prove and apply triangle theorem 2: (BC)^2 = (AC)^2 + (AB)^2 - 2(AB)(AD)
feedback:
In step \[3\], the Pythagorean theorem is being applied to \[ΔBCD\]. Therefore, in step \[4\], we should have the substitution step \[a^2=(c-x)^2+h^2 \].
The terms in the bracket are then expanded in step \[5\]: \[a^2=c^2-2cx+x^2+h^2\]. In step \[6\], we then substitute \[x^2+h^2\] with \[b^2\], so that we get \[a^2=c^2-2cx+b^2\]
Therefore, steps \[4\] and \[6\] have to be switched for the proof to be correct.

subject: Mathematics
q: Given a triangle with sides \[8\] cm, \[13\] cm, and \[15\] cm, what is the length of the longest median?
topic: Projection of a Side of a Triangle
skill: Prove and apply Apollonius' theorem: (AB)^2 + (AC)^2 = 2(BD)^2 + 2(AD)^2
feedback:
We know that each triangle has \[3\] medians. Let us calculate the lengths of these medians.
The first median can be drawn as follows:



To get \[AD\], we apply Apollonius's theorem as follows:

\begin{align}8^2+13^2&=2(7.5)^2+2(AD)^2 \\\\64+169&=2(56.25)+2(AD)^2\\\\233&=112.5 + 2(AD)^2\\\\233-112.5&=2(AD)^2\\\\\frac{120.5}{2}&=(AD)^2\\\\(AD)^2&=60.25\\\\⇒ AD &≈7.762 \: \mbox{cm}\end{align}

Following the same procedures, we can calculate the other two medians.



\begin{align}8^2+15^2&=2(6.5)^2+2(BE)^2 \\\\64+225&=2(42.25) +2(BE)^2\\\\289&=84.5+2(BE)^2\\\\289-84.5&=2(BE)^2\\\\\frac{204.5}{2}&=2(BE)^2\\\\(BE)^2&=102.25\\\\⇒BE & ≈10.11 \: \mbox{cm}\end{align}



\begin{align}13^3+15^2&=2(4)^2+2(CF)^2 \\\\169 + 225&=2(16)+2(CF)^2\\\\394-32&=2(CF)^2\\\\(CF)^2&=\frac{362}{2}\\\\(CF)^2&=181\\\\⇒CF&≈13.45 \: \mbox{cm}\end{align}

Therefore, the longest median is \[CF\] because it has a length of \[13.45\: \mbox{cm.}\]

subject: Mathematics
q:
David is trying to derive the relation \[(BC)^2=2(AC)(CD)\] by applying Apollonius's theorem to the following isosceles triangle, where \[BD⊥AC\]. What is the missing step?


Applying theorem 2 to \[ΔABC:\]
Step 1: \[(AB)^2=(AC)^2+(BC)^2-2(AC)(CD) \]
Step 2: \[?\]
Step 3: \[0=(BC)^2-2(AC)(CD) \]
Step 4: \[(BC)^2=2(AC)(CD)\]
topic: Projection of a Side of a Triangle
skill: Prove and apply Apollonius' theorem: (AB)^2 + (AC)^2 = 2(BD)^2 + 2(AD)^2
feedback:
The missing step is \[(AC)^2=(AC)^2+(BC)^2-2(AC)(CD)\]. In this step, we note that \[AB=AC\] since \[ΔABC\] is an isosceles triangle.

This allows us to cancel out the term \[(AC)^2\] on both sides to obtain step \[3\].

\begin{align}(AC)^2-(AC)^2&=(BC)^2-2(AC)(CD)\\\\0&=(BC)^2-2(AC)(CD)\\\\2(AC)(CD)&=(BC)^2 \end{align}

q:
Below is an incomplete proof that one and only one circle can pass through three non-collinear points. Determine which statements are correct.
Let \[A, B\] and \[C\] be any three non collinear points as shown in the diagram. Join \[A\] with \[B\] and \[B\] with \[C\], then draw \[\overline{DF}⊥\] bisector to \[\overline{AB}\], and \[\overline{EG}⊥\] bisector to \[\overline{BC}\]. Since \[\overline{DF}\] and \[\overline{EG}\] are not parallel, they intersect at point \[O\]. Also, join \[A, B\] and \[C\] with point \[O\].
Statement 1: Since \[\overline{DF}⊥\] bisector to \[\overline{AB}\] and point \[O\] lies on \[\overline{DF}\], we have \[OA=OB\].
Statement 2: Since \[ΔAOD\] is congruent to \[ΔOBD\], we have \[OA=OB\].
Statement 3: Since \[\overline{EG}⊥\] bisector to \[\overline{BC}\] and point \[O\] lies on \[\overline{EG}\], we have \[OB=OC\]. Thus, we have \[OA=OB=OC\].
Statement 4: Since \[ΔAOB\] is congruent to \[ΔCOB\], we have \[OA=OC\]. Thus, we have \[OA=OB=OC\].
skill: Prove and apply: One and only one circle can pass through three non-collinear points
feedback:
Option 1: Since \[\overline{DF}⊥\] bisector to \[\overline{AB}\], every point on \[\overline{DF}\] is equidistant from \[A\] and \[B\]. In particular, since point \[O\] lies on \[\overline{DF}\], we have \[OA=OB\]. Thus statement 1 is correct.

Since \[\overline{DF}⊥\] bisector to \[\overline{AB}\], we have \[AD=BD\]. Together with \[OA=OB\] and \[\overline{OD}\] being the common side to \[ΔAOD\] and \[ΔBOD, \; ΔAOD\] is congruent to\[ ΔBOD\]. Thus statement 2 is wrong.

Since \[\overline{EG}⊥\] bisector to \[\overline{BC}\], every point on \[\overline{EG}\] is equidistant from \[B\] and \[C.\] In particular, since point \[O\] lies on \[\overline{EG}\], we have \[OB=OC\]. Thus statement 3 is correct.

Statement 4 is incorrect, unless \[AB=BC\], which cannot be assumed.

q: \[A, B\] and \[C\] are three non-collinear points, and \[O\] is the centre of the circle that passes through them as shown in the diagram. q: \[AB\] and \[CD\] are two parallel chords of a circle (center at \[O\]) such that \[AE=BE=5 \: cm\] and \[CF=DF=12 \: cm\]. If the distance between the chords, \[EF\], is \[17 \: cm\], find the radius of the circle.
skill: Prove and apply: A straight line, drawn from the centre of a circle to bisect a chord (which is not a diameter) is perpendicular to the chord (and vice versa)
feedback:
Option 2: Let the length of \[OE\] be \[x\], and the radius of the circle be \[r\].

Since \[AE=BE\] and \[CF=DF\], theorem 2 tells us that both \[ΔOBE\] and \[ΔODF\] are right triangles. Applying Pythagorus theorem to these 2 triangles respectively give us:
\[r^2=x^2+5^2 ---- (1) \]
\[r^2=(17-x)^2+12^2 ---- (2)\]

We end up with a system of simultaneous equations in 2 variables.
Taking \[Eq(2) – Eq(1):\]
\[r^2-r^2=(17-x)^2+144-x^2-25\]
\[0=289+x^2-34x+144-x^2-25 \]
\[0=408-34x \]
\[⇒x=12\]

We can then use Eq(1) to find \[r\]:
\[r=\sqrt{x^2+25}=\sqrt{12^2+25}=13\: cm\]

q:
What is the next step in proving that a straight line drawn from the center of a circle to bisect a chord is perpendicular to that chord?

In \[ΔOAM↔ΔOBM\],
\[OA=OB \]
\[AM=BM \]
\[OM=OM\]
skill: Prove and apply: A straight line, drawn from the centre of a circle to bisect a chord (which is not a diameter) is perpendicular to the chord (and vice versa)
feedback:
Option 1: To prove that \[OM⊥AB\], we first need to show that \[ΔOAM\] and \[ΔOBM\] are congruent.

In the first few steps, we have already shown that the 3 corresponding sides of these 2 triangles are equal. This is sufficient proof that the triangles are congruent, so that the next logical step is to state this congruency, ie
\[\bigtriangleup OAM≅\bigtriangleup OBM \]

Further question:
After this, what is the sequence of options \[B\] to \[D\]?

q: In the diagram, \[O\] is the center of the circle. From the given dimensions, find \[UV.\]

feedback:
Option 3: Applying Pythagorus theorem to \[ΔOBV:\]
\[10^2=8^2+(OV)^2 \]
\[⇒V=6 \]
From theorem 3, we know that \[QU=SU\].
Applying Pythagorus theorem to \[ΔOSU:\]
\[10^2=5^2+(OU)^2 \]

Therefore,

subject: Mathematics
q: The triangle \[ABC\] and the right triangle \[BCD\] are drawn in a circle of radius \[r\] as shown. Given the following dimensions and that \[∠CAB\] is obtuse, find the distance of the chord \[\overline{BC}\] from the centre \[A\] of the circle.
skill: Prove and apply: A straight line, drawn from the centre of a circle to bisect a chord (which is not a diameter) is perpendicular to the chord (and vice versa)
feedback:
Option 1: First, we need to find \[r.\] Since \[∠CAB\] is an obtuse angle, using theorem \[1\] of chapter 8, we can write
\[(\sqrt{24})^2=r^2+r^2+2(r)(1) \]
\[2r^2+2r=24 \]
\[r^2+r=12\]
Solving this quadratic equation by completing the square:

\[r^2+r+\left ( \frac{1}{2} \right )^2=12+\left ( \frac{1}{2} \right )^2\]

\[\left ( r+\frac{1}{2} \right )^2=12+\frac{1}{4}\]

\[\left ( r+\frac{1}{2} \right )^2=\frac{49}{4}\]

\[⇒r+\frac{1}{2}=±\frac{7}{2}\]

\[r=-\frac{1}{2}+\frac{7}{2}=3\], or

\[r=-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{7}{2}=-4 \]

Rejecting the negative solution, we thus have
\[r=3 \: cm \]
\[ΔABC\] now looks like this:

Note that we have used theorem 3 to draw a perpendicular bisector from \[A\] to \[BC\]. Note also that the length of this bisector, \[d\], is the distance of chord \[\overline{BC}\] from \[A\].
Using Pythagoras’ theorem, we can write

\[d^2+\left ( \sqrt{\frac{24}{2}} \right )^2=3^2 \]

\[d^2+6=9 \]
\[d^2=3 \]
\[d=\sqrt{3}\: cm\]

subject: Mathematics
q: In the diagram below, \[O\] is the center of the circle. Find the area of \[ΔOAB\] in terms of \[r\] and \[θ\].
skill: Prove and apply: A straight line, drawn from the centre of a circle to bisect a chord (which is not a diameter) is perpendicular to the chord (and vice versa)
feedback:
Option 3: Using theorem 3, we can draw \[ΔOAB\] as follows:

Where \[h\] is the height and \[b\] is the base of the triangle.
From this, we can write

\[sin⁡θ=\frac{h}{r}\]

\[h=r(sin⁡θ) ---- (1) \]
Also,

\[cos⁡θ=\frac{\frac{b}{2}}{r} \]

\[b=2r(cos⁡θ) ---- (2) \]
The area of the triangle is given by
\[A=\frac{1}{2}(b)(h) \]
Substituting equations \[(1)\] and \[(2)\], we finally obtain

\[A=\frac{2r(cos⁡θ)(r)(sin⁡θ)}{2}\]

\[∴A=r^2 sin⁡θ\; cos⁡θ\]

subject: Mathematics
q: To prove that the straight line \[\overline{OM}\], which is perpendicular to the chord \[\overline{AB}\], bisects \[\overline{AB}\], we need to show that \[ΔOAM≅ΔOBM\]. Which postulate is used to demonstrate this congruency?
skill: Prove and apply: A straight line, drawn from the centre of a circle to bisect a chord (which is not a diameter) is perpendicular to the chord (and vice versa)
feedback:
Option 4: Since \[\overline{OA}\] and \[\overline{OB}\] are the radii of the same circle,
\[OA=OB ---- (1) \]
Since \[\overline{OM}\] is perpendicular to \[\overline{AB}\], we have
\[∠OMA=∠OMB=90° ---- (2) \]
Since \[\overline{OM}\] is common to both triangles, we have
\[OM=OM ---- (3) \]
Now, since the hypotenuse and one side of \[ΔOAM\] are equal to the corresponding hypotenuse and side of \[ΔOBM\], \[ΔOAM\] is congruent to \[ΔOBM\]. Thus, the \[H.S\] rule is used.

Important point:
Although the 2 theorems are related, do not confuse between their proving steps.

From the \[6\] chords shown in the diagram, only \[AC\] and \[GJ\] are equal in length. Therefore, they are the only chords that are equidistant from the centre.

q: In the following diagram, \[AB=CD\]. Which of the following statement is incorrect?
skill: Prove and apply: If two chords of a circle are congruent then they will be equidistant from the centre (and vice versa)
feedback:
It is not necessary that \[OH+OK > OD\]. We can always have a pair of congruent chords that are close to the center of the circle such that \[OH+OK < OD\].
Since \[AB=CD\], and these chords are respectively bisected by \[OH\] and \[OK\], we can conclude that \[AH=BH=CK=DK\].
\[OA\] and \[OC\] are the radii of the circle, hence they are equal to each other.
Since the chords \[OH\] and \[OK\] are congruent, they must be equidistant from the center of the circle, so this statement is correct as well.

subject: Mathematics
q: In the figure below, \[O\] is the center of the circle. Find the value of \[x\].
skill: Prove and apply: If two chords of a circle are congruent then they will be equidistant from the centre (and vice versa)
feedback:
Since the chords \[PQ\] and \[RS\] are congruent, they must be equidistant from the centre. Therefore:
\begin{align}4x+1&=2x+15 \\4x-2x&=15-1 \\2x&=14 \\x&=7\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: In this figure, \[AB=30\] cm, \[OM=20\] cm, \[ON=18\] cm, and \[O\] is the center of the circle. Find \[CN\].
skill: Prove and apply: If two chords of a circle are congruent then they will be equidistant from the centre (and vice versa)
feedback:
We first note that the chords \[AB\] and \[CD\] are not congruent because they are not equidistant from the center of the circle.
To find \[CN\], we make use of the fact that \[OA=OC\].
We then apply Pythagoras' theorem to \[ΔOAM\], to get:
\begin{align}(OA)^2&=(OM)^2+(AM)^2 \\(OA)^2&=20^2+15^2\\&=625\\\end{align}
Next, we apply Pythagoras' theorem to \[ΔOCN\], which gives us the correct answer:
\begin{align}(OC)^2&=(ON)^2+(CN)^2\\OC&=OA\\(OC)^2&=(OA)^2\\&=625\\625&=18^2+(CN)^2 \\CN&=\sqrt{625-18^2}\\&≈17.3\end{align}

subject: Mathematics
q: In the figure below, \[O\] is the center of the circle and \[AQ=11\]. Find \[RS\].
skill: Prove and apply: If two chords of a circle are congruent then they will be equidistant from the centre (and vice versa)
feedback:
Two chords of a circle which are equidistant from its centre must have the same length. Since \[OA=OB\], \[PQ\] and \[RS\] must also be equal.Therefore,
\begin{align}RS&=PQ\\&=2×AQ \\&=2×11\\&=22\end{align}

q:
We can see below that the largest chord in a circle is the diameter. Here, the circle is centered at \[O.\] What is the reason for the first statement?


\[OA+OB> AB \]                _____
\[OA+OB=CD\]                \[OA\] and \[OB\] are radii of the same circle.
\[∴CD> AB\]
options: The chord of a circle is always smaller than the diameter.
Two times the radius is equal to or larger than the diameter.
Sum of two sides of a triangle is greater than its third side.
The chord of a circle is always greater than the diameter.
skill: Prove and apply: If two chords of a circle are congruent then they will be equidistant from the centre (and vice versa)
feedback:
The reason for the first statement, \[OA+OB> AB \], is that the sum of two sides of a triangle is greater than its third side. This is a general statement that applies to all closed triangles.
The chord of a circle is NOT always smaller than the diameter because the diameter is also a chord.
\[2\] times the radius is always equal to the diameter.

subject: Mathematics
q:
We can see below that two chords of a circle which are equidistant from the center are congruent. Which step is wrong?

In \[∠rt\: Δ^s \: OAH↔OCK, \]
Step 1: \[OA=OC\]
Step 2: \[OH=OK \]
Step 3: \[ΔOAH≅ΔOCK \]

Step 4: Therefore, \[AH=\frac{1}{2} CD\]

Step 5: But \[AH=\frac{1}{2} AB \]

Step 6: Similarly, \[CK=\frac{1}{2} CD \]

Step 7: Since \[AH=CK \]

Step 8: \[∴\frac{1}{2}AB=\frac{1}{2} CD \]

Step 9: Or, \[AB=CD\]
skill: Prove and apply: If two chords of a circle are congruent then they will be equidistant from the centre (and vice versa)
feedback: Step 4, \[AH=\frac{1}{2} CD\], is incorrect. In Step 4, \[AH\] should be equal to \[CK\]. It is not possible to arrive at \[AH=\frac{1}{2} CD\] from the first few steps.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Which step is missing (denoted by ??) in the proof that two chords of a circle which are equidistant from the centre are congruent?

In \[∠rt Δ^s OAH↔OCK, \]
\[OA=OC\]                          Radii of the same circle
\[OH=OK \]                        Given

\[?? \]

Therefore,\[ AH=CK\]       Corresponding sides of congruent triangles

But \[AH=\frac{1}{2} AB\]                \[OH⊥AB\]

Similarly, \[CK=\frac{1}{2} CD \]     \[OK⊥CD\]

Since \[AH=CK \]

\[∴\frac{1}{2} AB=\frac{1}{2} CD \]

Or, \[AB=CD\]
skill: Prove and apply: If two chords of a circle are congruent then they will be equidistant from the centre (and vice versa)
feedback:
If we can show that \[ΔOAH\] and \[ΔOCK\] are congruent, then we can conclude that chords \[AB \] and \[CD\] are congruent.
In the first few steps, we have shown that the two corresponding sides of \[ΔOAH\] and \[ΔOCK\] are equal. Since these are right triangles, the \[HS\] postulate allows us to conclude that they are congruent in the next step, \[ΔOAH≅ΔOCK\].

subject: Mathematics
q: Let \[AB\] and \[CD\] be two chords which are equidistant from the centre of the circle and intersect at point \[E\] as shown in the figure. Given that the ratio of \[CE\] to \[DE\] is \[1:3\], \[AE=DE\] and \[BE=2\: cm\], find \[CD\].
skill: Prove and apply: If two chords of a circle are congruent then they will be equidistant from the centre (and vice versa)
feedback:
Since \[AB\] and \[CD\] are equidistant from the centre, they are congruent.
Hence,
\[AB=CD ---- (1)\]
We are given that \[AE=DE\] and if we make use of equation (1), we have
\[BE=AB-AE=CD-DE=CE \]
\[⇒BE=CE ---- (2) \]
Substituting \[BE=2\] in equation (2),
\[CE=2 ---- (3) \]
Given that \[CE\] to \[DE\] is \[1:3\], we have

\[\frac{CE}{DE}=\frac{1}{3}\]

\[DE=3CE ---- (4) \]
Using \[CE=2\], this becomes
\[DE=3(2)=6 ---- (5) \]
Thus,
\[CD=CE+DE\]
\[CD=2+6=8\:\] cm

subject: Mathematics
q: Let \[AB\] and \[AC\] be two congruent chords as shown in the figure. What is \[ΔAOD\] congruent to?
skill: Prove and apply: If two chords of a circle are congruent then they will be equidistant from the centre (and vice versa)
feedback:
Consider the correspondence between \[ΔAOD\] and \[ΔAOE\].
Since \[AO\] is common to both triangles, we have
\[AO=AO ---- (1) \]
Since \[AB\] and \[AC\] are congruent chords, according totheorem 4 they are equidistant from the centre
\[⇒OD=OE ---- (2) \]
Since \[OD⊥AB\], according to theorem 3 \[OD\] bisects \[AB\]

\[⇒DA=\frac{1}{2}AB ---- (3)\]

Similarly,

\[EA=\frac{1}{2} AC ---- (4) \]

Because \[AB\] and \[AC\] are congruent chords, we have
\[DA=EA ---- (5) \]
From equations \[(1)\], \[(2)\] and \[(5)\], since the three sides of \[ΔAOD\] are equal to the three sides of \[ΔAOE, ΔAOD\] is congruent to \[ΔAOE\].

Important point:
When writing a statement of congruency, make sure that the order of the letters in one triangle corresponds to the other. In this question, \[ΔAOD≅ΔAOE\], but \[ΔAOD\] and \[ΔOEA\] are not congruent.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let \[AB\] and \[CD\] be two chords of a circle with centre at \[O\] as shown in the diagram. To prove that they are congruent (given that they are equidistant from the centre), we need to show that \[ΔOAE≅ΔOCF\]. Which postulate will be used to demonstrate this?
skill: Prove and apply: If two chords of a circle are congruent then they will be equidistant from the centre (and vice versa)
feedback:
\[ΔOAE≅ΔOCF\] is proven by the \[HS\] postulate. Recall that in the proof, we show \[ΔOAE≅ΔOCF\] by stating that:
\[ΔOAE\] and \[ΔOCF\] are right triangles. We conclude this from the construction part of the theorem
\[OA=OC\] since both are radii of the same circle. This statement fulfills the hypotenuse requirement.
\[OE=OF\] since \[AB\] and \[CD\] are congruent. This statement fulfills the side requirement.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let \[AB\] and \[CD\] be two chords of a circle with centre at \[O\] as shown in the diagram. To prove that \[AB\] and \[CD\] are equidistant from the centre (given that they are congruent), we need to show that \[ΔOAE≅ΔOCF.\] Which postulate is used to demonstrate this?
skill: Prove and apply: If two chords of a circle are congruent then they will be equidistant from the centre (and vice versa)
feedback:
\[ΔOAE≅ΔOCF\] is proved by the \[HS\] postulate. Recall that in the proof, we show \[ΔOAE≅ΔOCF\] by stating that:
\[ΔOAE\] and \[ΔOCF\] are right triangles. We conclude this from the construction part of the theorem.
\[OA=OC\] since both are radii of the same circle. This statement fulfills the hypotenuse requirement.
\[AE=CF\] since \[AB\] and \[CD\] are both perpendicularly bisected. This statement fulfills the side requirement.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let \[AB\] and \[CD\] be two chords of a circle with centre at \[O\] as shown in the diagram. If they are equidistant from the centre, they are congruent. To prove this theorem, we need to show that _____.
skill: Prove and apply: If two chords of a circle are congruent then they will be equidistant from the centre (and vice versa)
feedback:
To prove this theorem, we must arrive at the conclusion that the chords are congruent. In other words, they are of the same length: \[AB=CD\].
Therefore, we start with the given condition that they are equidistant from the center: \[OE=OF\].
